en.extract:
The southern black flycatcher (Melaenornis pammelaina) is a small passerine bird of the genus Melaenornis in the flycatcher family, Muscicapidae, native to open and lightly wooded areas of eastern and southern Africa.
This species has a large range, with an estimated global extent of occurrence of 4,000,000 square kilometres (1,500,000 sq mi). The global population size has not been quantified but the bird is listed by the IUCN as being of "Least concern".


Description
The southern black flycatcher is entirely black, with a black beak and black legs. The iris of the eye is brown and this, along with the square-cut tail, helps distinguish it from the otherwise similar fork-tailed drongo (Dicrurus adsimilis) which has a red iris and long forked tail.


Distribution and habitat
The southern black flycatcher is native to eastern and southern Africa. It has been recorded from Somalia, Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania, Rwanda, Burundi, Congo, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Angola, Namibia, Eswatini, Botswana, Malawi, Mozambique, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Lesotho and South Africa. It is typically found in habitats with open areas and light woodland, riparian corridors, the edges of plantations and in gardens. The trees in these habitats include miombo (Brachystegia spp.), thorntrees (Acacia spp.) and mopane (Colosphermum mopane).


Behaviour
The southern black flycatcher is a mainly insectivorous bird. Its diet includes beetles, termites, locusts, worms, spiders and centipedes. Its main foraging technique is to perch on a low eminence such as a branch or fence post and pounce on prey in the air or on the ground below. It is also known to sip nectar from the flowers of mountain aloe (Aloe marlothii) and to eat the berries of the black nightshade (Solanum nigrum). It sometimes forages in small flocks, often associating with fork-tailed drongos. Possible benefits for this association is a lower predation risk and a higher foraging success.
Breeding takes place between May and January, peaking in September and October. The nest is usually built in a recess in a tree trunk or behind a loose slab of bark, but is sometimes in a tangle of creepers, the sheath of a palm frond, banana bunch or an abandoned farm implement. It is cup-shaped and composed of twigs and dry grasses, lined with fine rootlets. Up to four eggs are laid and incubation lasts about two weeks with the young fledging and leaving the nest in fifteen to twenty days.


Status
The IUCN list the southern black flycatcher as being of "Least concern". This is because it has a wide range, approximately 4,000,000 square kilometres (1,500,000 sq mi), the population seems stable and the bird is said to be common over at least part of its range.


References


External links

Southern black flycatcher - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.

The '''southern black flycatcher''' ('''''Melaenornis pammelaina''''') is a small [[passerine]] [[bird]] of the genus ''[[Melaenornis]]'' in the [[Old World flycatcher|flycatcher]] family, [[Muscicapidae]], native to open and lightly wooded areas of eastern and southern Africa.<ref>{{cite journal | url = http://www.hbw.com/species/southern-black-flycatcher-melaenornis-pammelaina | title = Southern Black-flycatcher (Melaenornis pammelaina) | journal = Birds of the World | date = 2020 | last1 = Taylor | first1 = Barry }}</ref><ref>[http://sabap2.adu.org.za/docs/sabap1/694.pdf Melaenornis pammelaina]</ref>

This species has a large range, with an estimated global extent of occurrence of {{convert|4000000|km2}}. The global population size has not been quantified but the bird is listed by the [[IUCN]] as being of "Least concern".

==Description==
The southern black flycatcher is entirely black, with a black beak and black legs. The iris of the eye is brown and this, along with the square-cut tail, helps distinguish it from the otherwise similar [[fork-tailed drongo]] (''Dicrurus adsimilis'') which has a red iris and long forked tail.{{cn|date=January 2025}}

==Distribution and habitat==
The southern black flycatcher is native to eastern and southern Africa. It has been recorded from Somalia, Kenya, Uganda, Tanzania, Rwanda, Burundi, Congo, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Angola, Namibia, Eswatini, Botswana, Malawi, Mozambique, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Lesotho and South Africa.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /> It is typically found in habitats with open areas and light woodland, riparian corridors, the edges of plantations and in gardens. The trees in these habitats include [[Brachystegia|miombo]] (''Brachystegia'' spp.), [[Acacia|thorntrees]] (''Acacia'' spp.) and [[mopane]] (''Colosphermum mopane'').<ref name=Izico>{{cite web |url=http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/muscicapidae/melaenornis_pammelaina.htm |title=''Melaenornis pammelaina'' (Southern black flycatcher) |work=Biodiversity Explorer |publisher=Izico |access-date=2014-06-07}}</ref>

==Behaviour==
The southern black flycatcher is a mainly [[Insectivore|insectivorous]] bird. Its diet includes [[beetle]]s, [[termite]]s, [[locust]]s, [[worm]]s, [[spider]]s and [[centipede]]s. Its main foraging technique is to perch on a low eminence such as a branch or fence post and pounce on prey in the air or on the ground below. It is also known to sip [[nectar]] from the flowers of [[Aloe marlothii|mountain aloe]] (''Aloe marlothii'') and to eat the berries of the [[Solanum nigrum|black nightshade]] (''Solanum nigrum''). It sometimes forages in small flocks, often associating with fork-tailed drongos.<ref name=Izico/><ref>{{cite journal |author=Fraser, Warwick |year=1983 |title=Foraging patterns of some South African flycatchers |journal=Ostrich: Journal of African Ornithology |volume=54 |issue=3 |pages=150–155 |doi=10.1080/00306525.1983.9634464 |bibcode=1983Ostri..54..150F }}</ref> Possible benefits for this association is a lower predation risk and a higher foraging success.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Morgan |first1=Tc |last2=McCleery |first2=Ra |last3=Moulton |first3=Mp |last4=Monadjem |first4=A |date=August 2012 |title=Are Southern Black Flycatchers Melaenornis pammelaina associated with Fork-tailed Drongos Dicrurus adsimilis ? |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.2989/00306525.2012.690351 |journal=Ostrich |language=en |volume=83 |issue=2 |pages=109–111 |doi=10.2989/00306525.2012.690351 |bibcode=2012Ostri..83..109M |s2cid=83726699 |issn=0030-6525|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Breeding takes place between May and January, peaking in September and October. The nest is usually built in a recess in a tree trunk or behind a loose slab of bark, but is sometimes in a tangle of creepers, the sheath of a palm frond, banana bunch or an abandoned farm implement. It is cup-shaped and composed of twigs and dry grasses, lined with fine rootlets. Up to four eggs are laid and incubation lasts about two weeks with the young [[Fledge|fledging]] and leaving the nest in fifteen to twenty days.<ref name=Izico/>

==Status==
The [[IUCN]] list the southern black flycatcher as being of "[[Least concern]]". This is because it has a wide range, approximately {{convert|4000000|km2}}, the population seems stable and the bird is said to be common over at least part of its range.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=6469 |title=Southern Black Flycatcher ''Melaenornis pammelaina'' |year=2014 |publisher=BirdLife International |access-date=2014-06-07}}</ref>
